formulas.name: First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)
formulas.notes: When an object is in a state of rest or uniform motion, it will remain in that state unless an external force affects it.
formulas.formula: An object at rest will remain at rest, and an object in motion will continue to move with a constant velocity, unless acted upon by an external force.

formulas.name: Second Law of Motion
formulas.notes: When an external force is applied to an object, the resulting acceleration is directly proportional to the force and inversely proportional to the object's mass.
formulas.units: F in Newtons (N), m in kilograms (kg), a in meters per second squared (m/s^2)

variables.F: Net force acting on an object

formulas.name: Third Law of Motion (Law of Action and Reaction)
formulas.notes: When two objects interact, they apply forces to one another that are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
formulas.formula: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

formulas.name: Force and Acceleration Relation
formulas.notes: This is a rearrangement of the second law of motion, showing that acceleration is directly proportional to the net force applied and inversely proportional to the object's mass.
formulas.units: a in meters per second squared (m/s^2), F in Newtons (N), m in kilograms (kg)

variables.F: Net force acting on the object

formulas.name: Work and Force Relation
formulas.notes: This formula calculates the work done on an object by a force, taking into account the force's magnitude, direction, and the object's displacement.
formulas.units: W in Joules (J), F in Newtons (N), d in meters (m), θ in radians

variables.F: Net force acting on the object
variables.d: Distance moved by the object in the direction of the force
variables.θ: Angle between the force and the direction of motion

formulas.name: Work and Energy Relation
formulas.notes: This formula states that the work done on an object is equal to the change in its kinetic energy, assuming no other forms of energy are involved.
formulas.units: W in Joules (J), ΔE in Joules (J)

variables.ΔE: Change in the object's kinetic energy

formulas.name: Impulse and Momentum Relation
formulas.notes: This formula relates the impulse applied to an object to the resulting change in its momentum.
formulas.units: J in Newton-seconds (N*s), Δp in kg*m/s

formulas.name: Impulse and Force Relation
formulas.notes: This formula calculates the impulse applied to an object by a force, taking into account the force's magnitude and the duration of its application.
formulas.units: J in Newton-seconds (N*s), F in Newtons (N), Δt in seconds (s)

variables.F: Net force acting on the object
variables.Δt: Time over which the force is applied

formulas.name: Impulse and Momentum Relation (alternative form)
formulas.notes: This formula relates the impulse applied to an object to the resulting change in its momentum, using the force and time over which it is applied.
formulas.units: F in Newtons (N), Δt in seconds (s), Δp in kg*m/s

variables.F: Net force acting on the object
variables.Δt: Time over which the force is applied

formulas.name: Momentum and Velocity Relation
formulas.notes: This formula shows that the momentum of an object is directly proportional to its mass and velocity.
formulas.units: p in kg*m/s, m in kilograms (kg), v in meters per second (m/s)
